coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and gold nanoparticles based “sandwich” type immunoassay for sensitive and selective carcinoembryonic antigen detection

coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Yuanping
Xu, Wenjun
Zhu, Minjie
Zhang, Zongwen
Peng, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: In this article, a simple immunoassay was developed for the highly sensitive and selective detection of biomarker carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) for early cancer diagnosis. The platform was constructed from CEA antibody-modified carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), in which the two nanomaterials would form a sandwich-like structure and result an enhanced fluorescence in the presence of the target analyte CEA. Under physiological conditions, the assay exhibited a wide linear range (0–466 ng/mL) and an extremely low detection limit (1.49 × 10−5 ng/mL), placing it among the most sensitive sensors for CEA detection. Furthermore, the assay also demonstrated high selectivity towards CEA free from the interference of majority of common biologically relevant species. Most importantly, the assay functioned well in serum environment, demonstrating high potential for clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carcinoembryonic antigen
Immune sensing
Fluorescence sensing
Cancer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of CDs with CEA Ab
Conjugation of AuNPs with CEA Ab
Development of immunoassay for CEA detection

Peng would like to express gratitude for the funding received from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under the grant number 22465034, the High-Level Talents Introduction Program in Yunnan Province (No. C619300A081) and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province (grant number: 202301AT070147). M. Zhu and Z. Peng thank the generous support from the Graduate Research Innovation Project of Yunnan University (KC-23234806).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs. In the upper right corner is the lattice fringe pattern of a specific particle; and the left inset is the histogram of the particle size distribution. (b) XRD spectrum of CDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line), fluorescent excitation spectrum (red line, λem = 540 nm), and fluorescent emission spectrum (blue line, λex = 360 nm) of aqueous dispersion of CDs (1 mg/mL). The insets are the photographs of the CDs dispersion under ambient light (left) and excited by a 365 nm ultraviolet light (right). (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs dispersion excited by light of different wavelengths as labeled. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The FTIR spectra of the raw materials (red) and CDs (black); (b) the XPS full spectrum of CDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) C 1s, (f) Br 3d. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a), (b) and (c) Schematic representations of the construction of a sandwich type immunoassay based on CDs and AuNPs for CEA detection; (d) potential fluorescence emission alterations upon interaction of CDs with AuNPs; (e) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of IgG of different concentrations (0–5940 ng/mL); (f) calibration curve established for IgG in the range of 0–5940 ng/mL. Where F and F0 are the emission intensities of CDs at 540 nm in the presence and absence of IgG, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CEA Ab-CDs; (b) zeta potentials of CDs, CEA Ab and CEA Ab-CDs, respectively; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs, CEA Ab and CEA Ab-AuNPs, respectively; (d) zeta potentials of AuNPs, CEA Ab and CEA Ab-AuNPs, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing assay under 360 nm excitation in the presence of CEA of varied concentrations (0–833 ng/mL); (b) relationship between the concentrations of CEA and the fluorescence intensities of sensing assay in the range of 0–833 ng/mL; (c) calibration curve of the sensing assay, with a linear range of 0–466 ng/mL; (d) normalized fluorescence intensities of the assay at 540 nm in the presence of different interfering biomolecules (from left to right, blank solution, CEA, BSA, FA, AA, Glc, L-Glu, L-Arg, and IgG) and a mixture of different ions including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Fe3+.
Recoveries and RSDs of CEA solutions in PBS buffer and serum.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and gold nanoparticles based “sandwich” type immunoassay for sensitive and selective carcinoembryonic antigen detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology, National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials, School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology
National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Key Laboratory for Micro/Nano Materials & Technology, National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials, School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electron Microscopy Center, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electron Microscopy Center, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, a simple immunoassay was developed for the highly sensitive and selective detection of biomarker carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) for early cancer diagnosis. The platform was constructed from CEA antibody-modified carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), in which the two nanomaterials would form a sandwich-like structure and result an enhanced fluorescence in the presence of the target analyte CEA. Under physiological conditions, the assay exhibited a wide linear range (0–466 ng/mL) and an extremely low detection limit (1.49 × 10−5 ng/mL), placing it among the most sensitive sensors for CEA detection. Furthermore, the assay also demonstrated high selectivity towards CEA free from the interference of majority of common biologically relevant species. Most importantly, the assay functioned well in serum environment, demonstrating high potential for clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is among the leading causes of morbidity and mortality worldwide [1,2]. Early diagnosis of cancer is considered a golden step towards timely treatment [3], which not only increases the success rate of treatment but also reduces economic costs and health burdens. Detection of biological markers that are specific to tumors is considered to be the most prominent method for the early diagnosis of cancer [4]. Among the various biomarkers, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) has been associated with various cancers including lung cancer, breast cancer, gastric cancer as well as colon cancer [5], and has been widely regarded as a promising target for early screening of tumors. As such, development of sensing assays that are simple in design, easy to operate, and highly sensitive for CEA detection is of great importance.

Recent studies have reported the practicality of fluorescent probes in detecting biomarkers (i.e., CEA), which has low cost, ease of operation, high sensitivity, and real-time detection [6,7]. In this context, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention thanks to their excellent photoluminescence characteristics [8,9], low toxicity, good water dispersibility, and high biocompatibility [10–12]. However, research and application of CDs-based fluorescent probes for CEA detection was still in their infancy, even though a few studies have been sporadically reported (Table S1, entries 15–19). For example, Miao et al. used tomato juice as a carbon source and successfully realized the detection of CEA in the range of 1–5 × 105 ng/mL via an “on-off-on” approach [13] (Table S1, entry 15). On the other hand, Xiang et al. designed a fluorescent CDs biosensor via click reactions, and achieved the selective sensing of CEA with a low detection of limit (LOD) of 0.00732 ng/mL [14] (Table S1, entry 17). In addition, Wang et al. prepared CDs@SiO2 nanorods for the detection of CEA under FL-IRA dual-mode conditions [15] (Table S1, entry 19).

Although these existing analytical methods have achieved initial success in CEA detection, they generally faced some significant limitations. Firstly, the sensing designs of these methods are usually complex, leaving the probes difficult to operate and generalize in practice (Table S1, entries 1–14 and 17–19) [2,14–29]. Secondly, most of the current assays exhibited very narrow linear response ranges, which significantly limited their practical applications (Table S1, entries 1–5, 13–14 and 16–18) [14,16–20,25–27,30]. Thirdly, majority of the current assays demonstrated very limited LODs, although most of them claimed their LOD values were already lower than the normal CEA concentration (20 ng/mL) in serum (Table S1, entries 2–7, 9–14 and 15–17) [2,13,14,17–22,24,26–30]. However, from the practical clinical and early screening point of view, the ideal CEA assay better have a much lower detection threshold in order to accurately capture small changes in CEA concentration at even lower concentration levels [28,29]. As such, it is still of great challenge and importance to develop CEA sensing assays with simple design strategy, wider linear range and even lower LODs, which is particularly urgent in the pursuit of earlier warning and refined diagnosis and treatment.

In this context, we report here a CDs-based assay that achieved the super sensitive detection of CEA. To the best of our knowledge, this assay is the first example of a “sandwich” sensor constructed from CDs and Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) for CEA detection (Table S1). The CDs were prepared via microwave irradiation using 2,5-dibromoterephthalic acid as the carbon precursor and 1,4-cyclohexanediamine as the nitrogen doping agent. The sandwich-type immune-sensing assay was then constructed exploiting the delicate interaction between CEA antibody-functionalized (Ab-functionalized) CDs and AuNPs. In the presence of the target CEA molecules, the highly specific interaction between CEA and CEA Ab brought CDs and AuNPs to each other’s vicinity, which led to a metal-induced fluorescence enhancement on CDs [31], thereby achieving the sensitive and selective detection of the biomarker CEA. Specifically, the assay achieved a broad linear range of 0–466 ng/mL and an extremely low LOD of 1.49 × 10−5 ng/mL, which is not only adequate to cover the CEA levels of both healthy individuals and patients, but also surpassed majority of the reported assays (Table S1). Furthermore, the assay also exhibited excellent specificity towards CEA, free from the interfering of seven biologically common biomolecules and five metal ions. Most importantly, it demonstrated high reliability towards CEA sensing in both PBS buffer and fetal bovine serum (FBS), highlighting its potential application in clinical diagnosis.

CDs applied in this study were prepared from 2,5-dibromoterephthalic acid via simple microwave irradiation. Specifically, 2,5-dibromoterephthalic acid (0.324 g) and trans-1,4-diaminocyclohexane (0.342 g) were dissolved in 3 mL of deionized (DI) water and sonicated for 5 min. Then, the mixture was placed in a microwave synthesizer (model: Discover SP of American CEM; power: 200 W, the temperature and pressure were set at 200 °C and 250 PSI, respectively) and irradiated for 20 min. After the reaction was complete, the reaction mixture was dialyzed for 6 h, and then lyophilized to obtain the final product in the form of a yellow powder.

AuNPs used in this study were synthesized by following a previously reported study with slight modification [4]. Specifically, 50 mL of 0.01 % (w/v) tetrachloroauric acid solution in DI water was placed in a round bottom flask and heated to boiling, upon which 2 mL of 1 % (w/v) sodium citrate was added all at once with the mixture was being continuously heated and swirled using a magnetic stirrer. When the solution turned into a translucent red hue, the heating was halted, and the reaction was then cooled in dark. After the solution was cooled down to r.t, it was diluted to 50 mL, and stored in dark at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of CDs with CEA Ab
ce:section.ce:para: To start with, 200 μL of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 100 mM) was added to 2 mL of CDs dispersion (1 mg/mL), and the mixture was agitated 15 mins before 200 μL of N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 25 mM) was added. The whole mixture was stirred for 45 min at r.t., after which, 200 μL of CEA Ab solution was added and the reaction was further agitated for 2 h at r.t. When the reaction was done, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove any large particles, and the supernatant was collected and stored in dark at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of AuNPs with CEA Ab
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 2 mL of AuNPs dispersion (pH = 9) and 400 μL of CEA Ab were stirred at r.t. in dark continuously for 120 min, after which the resulted mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The precipitates were collected, re-dispersed in PBS buffer and stored in dark at 4 °C for further use.

100 μL of CEA Ab-CDs and 40 μL of CEA Ab-AuNPs were mixed thoroughly to yield the initial sensing assay, and then series of antigen solutions of varied concentrations were added. The mixtures were incubated for a certain time (15, 30 and 60 min, respectively) before their fluorescence emissions at 540 nm were monitored. Ultimately, the idea incubation time was ascertained by comparing the R2 values of the calibration curves at various intervals with the fluorescence recovery values, which was determined as 30 min.

To the mixture of CEA Ab-CDs (100 μL) and CEA Ab-AuNPs (40 μL), a certain concentration of antigen was added and the whole mixture was thoroughly mixed and incubated at r.t. for 30 min, after which the fluorescence emission intensity at 540 nm was measured and recorded. The process was repeated for antigen of gradient concentrations, and a calibration curve showing the fluorescence intensities versus the concentrations of antigen was established.

The LOD of the assay was determined by the following equation:
LOD =
3 σ
k
where σ is the standard deviation of at least 20 blank samples. In other words, one needs to prepare 20 samples of CDs solution without the test substance (a mixture of CEA Ab-CDs and CEA Ab-AuNPs), test their fluorescence intensities at 540 nm emission under the same conditions, and then calculate the standard deviation of these 20 fluorescence intensity values. k is the slope of the linear calibration curve [32,33].

To test the specificity of the assay towards CEA antigen, 0.33 μg/mL of interfering species including bovine serum albumin (BSA), folic acid (FA), L-ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glc), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), L-arginine (L-Arg) and a mixture of common inorganic ions including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+ were added to the sensing assay containing CEA Ab-CDs and CEA Ab-AuNPs individually, the samples were then incubated at r.t. for 30 min and their fluorescence intensities at 540 nm were measured to evaluate the interference of the substances towards the assay.

To probe the reliability of the assay, five spiked CEA solutions of varied concentrations were prepared in PBS buffer and fetal bovine serum environment, respectively, and their concentrations were determined based on the established calibration curve, which were subsequently compared to their actual values to calculate the recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSD).

Specifically,
R e c o v e r y
%
=
M e a s u r e d
C E A
c o n c e n t r a t i o n
A d d e d
C E A
c o n c e n t r a t i o n
× 100 % ;
R S D
%
=
σ A
× 100 %
where A and σ are the mean and standard deviation of three measurements at a certain CEA concentration, respectively.

The CDs reported in this study were obtained as yellow powders after simple microwave treatment of the carbon precursor 2,5-dibromoterephthalic acid. The morphological, structural, optical and chemical properties of the samples were then carefully examined by TEM, XRD, UV–vis, fluorescence, FTIR and XPS. It could be seen that the CDs are uniformly dispersed spherical particles without any evident agglomeration (Fig. 1
a). The histogram of the particle size distributions revealed that the diameters of the CDs ranged from 2.0 to 4.0 nm, with an average size of 3.0 nm (Fig. 1 a, lower right inset). The CDs feature significant lattice striations with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1 a, upper right inset), which could be attributed to the (100) lattice spacing of graphite [34,35]. The CDs were further characterized with XRD to probe their structural features (Fig. 1 b). As shown, the samples revealed a broad diffraction peak centered at 21.1°, which is typical for CDs and normally served as the evidence of the successful carbonization and preparation of CDs [36].

Next, the optical properties of the sample were further investigated. The aqueous dispersion of CDs was colorless and transparent under natural light (Fig. 1 c, left inset), and turned to bright yellow upon excited by a 365 nm handheld UV lamp (Fig. 1 c, right inset). In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, three different characteristic peaks were observed (Fig. 1 c, black curve): the absorption peak at 258 nm is probably due to the π-π* transition of C=C [10], the absorption peak at 314 nm might be due to the n-π* transition of C=O/C=N, while the peak at 350 nm could be the core absorption of CDs [37]. As expected, the optimum excitation wavelength of the CDs was found at 360 nm (Fig. 1 c, red curve), which overlapped well with their UV–vis absorption. The maximum fluorescent emission of the sample located at 540 nm (Fig. 1 c, blue curve), which was indeed in the yellow light region. Surprisingly, the sample did not exhibit the typical excitation-wavelength-dependent emission behavior; instead, the maximum peak positions and peak shapes under different exciting wavelengths were very similar (Fig. 1 d).

Following the optical characterizations, the chemical compositions of the sample were further investigated by FTIR and XPS. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2
a), the peaks of the vicinity of 2880 cm−1 and 735–902 cm−1 were denoted to the vibrations of C–H; and peaks at 1556–1693, 1211–1475, and 1030 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C=C, C=N/C–N, C–O respectively. Furthermore, the peaks at 477–635 cm−1 might be due to the stretching vibrations of C–Br. Interestingly, CDs and the carbon precursor 2,5-dibromoterephthalic acid exhibited extremely similar absorption peaks, implying that CDs might have inherited majority of their surface functionalities from the precursor. Surprisingly, we did not observe the characteristic peak for the C=O bond (1693 cm−1) in the CDs, which might have been masked by the strong peak of the C=C bond [38].

In the full XPS spectrum, four peaks were observed at 70.8, 284.6, 402.6, and 532.5 eV, which corresponded to Br 3d, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 2 b). There were three peaks in the high-resolution spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 2 c), which were assigned to C=O (530.9 eV), C–O/C–OH (531.9 eV), and –OH (532.8 eV). Similarly, the high-resolution spectrum of N 1s showed two peaks at 399.1 and 401.1 eV, which were associated with the graphitic and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. 2 d). In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s, three different types of carbon were observed, namely C–C/C=C (284.8 eV), C–N (286.0 eV) and C=N/C=O (288.1 eV) (Fig. 2 e). In addition, in the high-resolution spectrum of Br 3d, Br existed in three forms as Br− (70.3 eV), C–Br 3d3/2 (71.0 eV), and C–Br 3d5/2 (71.5 eV), respectively (Fig. 2 f) [39]. In conclusion, the XPS characterization results were in general agreement with the FTIR analysis.

To realize the sensitive detection of CEA, a CDs-based, AuNPs assisted sandwich-type immunosensor was designed (Fig. 3 ). In this specific assay, both CDs and AuNPs are first functionalized with CEA-specific antibodies, resulting in the formation of antibody-coupled CDs (Ab-CDs) and antibody-labeled AuNPs (Ab-AuNPs), respectively (Fig. 3 a). For the functionalization of CDs, EDC-based covalent bio-conjugation was applied while for AuNPs, the strong surface energy of AuNPs were utilized to form a noncovalent bond between CEA and AuNPs. In the absence of the target antigen (i.e., CEA), there should be no (or only weak) interaction between CEA-CDs and CEA-AuNPs (Fig. 3 b). However, when the target antigen is present, the specific interaction between the antigen and the antibody could bring AuNPs close to CDs (Fig. 3 c). Therefore, the local surface plasmon of AuNPs overlaps with the emission spectrum of CDs (Fig. S1), which leads to phenomena such as fluorescence enhancement (Fig. 3 d, I) through enhanced absorption and radiative rate, or fluorescence quenching (Fig. 3 d, II) through energy transfer, or shift of emission peak positions (Fig. 3 d, III) [40,41]. Based on the altered fluorescent signals, the presence of CEA could be detected and quantitatively analyzed.

To establish the proof-of-principle: we first tested the possibility of constructing such sandwich-type immunosensor with IgG considering its wide availability and low cost. In principle, to achieve the alteration of PL of CDs by the interaction with AuNPs, there should be energy transfer between the two that require their absorption spectra have significant amount of overlap. As such, based on Fig. 1c, the AuNPs prepared should have strong absorptions in the region from 400–650 nm. To our delight, we successfully synthesized near spherical AuNPs (Fig. S2a) with an average diameter of 22.6 nm (Fig. S2b) using citrate reduction method, and the AuNPs indeed exhibited strong absorptions from 450 to 600 nm (Fig. S1, red curve). Subsequently, we coupled rabbit anti-human IgG (IgG Ab) with CDs and AuNPs through covalent/electrostatic interactions. Under the optimized conditions as described in the experiment section, we successfully built the desired “sandwich” prototype biosensor. To our delight, as the IgG concentrations gradually increased, the emission intensities of the CDs at 540 nm also increased accordingly (Fig. 3 e). In the range of 0–5940 ng/mL, there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescent emission intensities of CDs and the IgG concentrations, with R2 = 0.98 (Fig. 3 f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of immunoassay for CEA detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Exploiting the above-developed “sandwich” type sensor for IgG, we deliberately expanded its application to CEA detection. Specifically, we replaced the IgG Ab immobilized on CDs and AuNPs with CEA-specific antibody (CEA Ab), a process that involved the same chemistry as in binding IgG to CDs and AuNPs. Upon binding, it was found that the emission peak of CDs stayed the same as in peak positions from their original emission; however, a slight decrease in PL intensity was observed (Fig. 4
a). Further tests showed that the zeta potential of unmodified CDs was −2.41 mV (Fig. 4 b, red column), whereas that of CEA Ab-modified CDs was drastically reduced to −11.17 mV (Fig. 4 b, blue column). This significant change in zeta potential implied that CEA antibody successfully bind to CDs, and the presence of the antibody altered the surface charge distribution of CDs [42].

Similarly, a slight red shift from 523 to 534 nm in the maximum UV–vis absorption peak of AuNPs was observed (Fig. 4 c), which could be attributed to the change in the refractive index of AuNPs as a result of protein layer attachment [6]. The clear absorption peak at 280 nm (Fig. 4 c, red curve) corresponding to the characteristic absorption of CEA antibody could serve as further evidence of the successful coupling of CEA Ab with AuNPs. In addition, the slight positive zeta potential of the AuNPs (0.08 mV, Fig. 4 d, red column) was shifted to a negative value (−5.06 mV, Fig. 4 d, blue column) upon conjugated with CEA Ab, which could be attributed to the formation of stable binding complex between the two.

With the successful preparation of the CEA Ab-modified CDs and AuNPs, we then carefully optimized the incubation time for the assay (Fig. S3), which was determined to be 30 min. Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence emission intensities of the sensing assay exhibited a synchronized increasing trend with the CEA concentrations (Fig. 5
a); based on which a plot showing fluorescence intensities in the Y-axis and CEA concentrations in the X-axis was obtained (Fig. 5 b). Clearly, the plot demonstrated as an excellent linear line in the range from 0 to 466 ng/mL, with a R2 value of 0.98 (Fig. 5 c), highlighting the strong correlation between the fluorescence intensities and CEA concentrations. According to the well accepted 3σ method [43], the LOD of the assay was determined to be 1.49 × 10−5 ng/mL, which was much lower than the normal CEA level in serum (20 ng/mL) and surpassed the performances of majority of the reported assays (Table S1).

To rigorously evaluate the specificity of this assay towards CEA, the potential interfering effects of biomolecules (i.e., BSA, FA, AA, Glc, L-Glu, L-Arg, and IgG) commonly found in blood serum and metal ions (i.e., K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Fe3+) that may exist in physiological environments were carefully investigated. To our delight, the experimental results shown that only the target molecule CEA could induce a significant enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of the assay (Fig. 5 d, black column vs. red column). All the interfering species tested did not cause any similar trend of enhanced fluorescence response (Fig. 5 d), highlighting the high selectivity of the assay towards the targeted analyte CEA. The high selectivity of the assay could be attributed to the highly specific interaction between CEA and CEA antibody, which ensured the efficient and selective detection of CEA in complex samples.

Encouraged by the high sensitivity and selectivity, we further evaluated the reliability of the assay with five spiked CEA solutions in PBS buffer. Excitingly, the assay demonstrated high reliability towards all the five samples, in which all the recoveries were above 95 % and RSDs were less than 5 % (Table 1 , entries 1–5). Specifically, when the spiked concentration was 100.0 ng/mL, an excellent recovery of 97.7 % with an extremely low RSD of 0.1 % was achieved (Table 1, entry 1). To simulate real sensing environment, the practicality of the assay was further validated with CEA solutions in fetal bovine serum. It is well known that serum contains a large number of complex biomolecules such as proteins, peptides, antibodies, sugars, and etc [37], which means that the optimum sensing assay of PBS buffer does not necessarily preserve the same level of performance in a serum environment. To our delight, the assay maintained the same degree of performance towards all the five spiked solutions in fetal bovine serum; the recoveries were ranged from 91.4 to 105.3 % with RSDs from 0.9 to 5.6 % (Table 1, entries 6–10). In particular, for the sample with a concentration of 233.3 ng/mL, a superior recovery of 99.4 % with an excellent RSD of 0.9 % was achieved (Table 1, entry 9). These results strongly corroborate that the assay indeed maintains excellent reliability and accuracy in a serum background, thus providing a solid foundation for the accurate determination of CEA in real biological samples.

In this report, a highly sensitive and selective “sandwich” type assay for CEA sensing was developed from CEA Ab-functionalized CDs and AuNPs by exploiting the metal-induced fluorescence enhancement phenomenon. The assay achieved a broad linear response to CEA concentrations from 0 to 466 ng/mL, with an extremely low LOD of 1.49 × 10−5 ng/mL, which is far below the normal level of CEA in serum and significantly better than most existing methods. In addition, the method also demonstrated a high degree of selectivity and reliability, with excellent performance not only in PBS buffer, but also in more complex fetal bovine serum samples with very high recoveries, proving its great potential in clinical diagnosis. Given these advantages, future research will therefore focus on optimizing the method for more complex human sample environments, particularly to improve the sensitivity and specificity of CEA detection in human blood samples. We will also continue to explore possibility to simplify the operational process to promote its practical application in the field of clinical diagnostics. In conclusion, the novel fluorescence immunoassay proposed in this study not only exhibits superior technical performance, but also possesses a simple and economical design concept, which provides a new solution for the efficient detection of CEA and demonstrates a broad application prospect.

Yuanping Luo: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Wenjun Xu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Minjie Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Zongwen Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhili Peng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Z. Peng would like to express gratitude for the funding received from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under the grant number 22465034, the High-Level Talents Introduction Program in Yunnan Province (No. C619300A081) and the Applied Basic Research Program of Yunnan Province (grant number: 202301AT070147). M. Zhu and Z. Peng thank the generous support from the Graduate Research Innovation Project of Yunnan University (KC-23234806).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113388.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzyme-encapsulated protein trap engineered metal–organic framework-derived biomineral probes for non-invasive prostate cancer surveillance
